From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Extremely large or complex datasets

Not to be confused with [Big Data (band)](/wiki/Big_Data_(band) "Big Data (band)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Revised_NIST_Big_Data_Taxonomy.jpg/500px-Revised_NIST_Big_Data_Taxonomy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Revised_NIST_Big_Data_Taxonomy.jpg)

A diagram of the generation and common application of big data

**Big data** primarily refers to [data sets](/wiki/Data_set "Data set") that are too large or complex to be dealt with by traditional [data-processing](/wiki/Data_processing "Data processing") [software](/wiki/Application_software "Application software"). Data with many entries (rows) offers greater [statistical power](/wiki/Statistical_power "Statistical power"), while data with higher complexity (more attributes or columns) may lead to a higher [false discovery rate](/wiki/False_discovery_rate "False discovery rate").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Big data analysis challenges include [capturing data](/wiki/Automatic_identification_and_data_capture "Automatic identification and data capture"), [data storage](/wiki/Computer_data_storage "Computer data storage"), [data analysis](/wiki/Data_analysis "Data analysis"), search, [sharing](/wiki/Data_sharing "Data sharing"), [transfer](/wiki/Data_transmission "Data transmission"), [visualization](/wiki/Data_visualization "Data visualization"), [querying](/wiki/Query_language "Query language"), updating, [information privacy](/wiki/Information_privacy "Information privacy"), and data sources. Big data was originally associated with three key concepts: *volume*, *variety*, and *velocity*.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The analysis of big data that have only volume, velocity, and variety can pose challenges in [sampling](/wiki/Sampling_(statistics) "Sampling (statistics)"). A fourth concept, *veracity,* which refers to the level of reliability of data, was thus added[[3]](#cite_note-3). Without sufficient investment in expertise to ensure big data veracity, the volume and variety of data can produce costs and risks that exceed an organization's capacity to create and capture *value* from big data*.*[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Current usage of the term *big data* tends to refer to the use of [predictive analytics](/wiki/Predictive_analytics "Predictive analytics"), [user behavior analytics](/wiki/User_behavior_analytics "User behavior analytics"), or certain other advanced data analytics methods that extract [value](/wiki/Data_valuation "Data valuation") from big data, and seldom to a particular size of data set. "There is little doubt that the quantities of data now available are indeed large, but that's not the most relevant characteristic of this new data ecosystem."[[5]](#cite_note-5)
Analysis of data sets can find new correlations to "spot business trends, prevent diseases, combat crime and so on".[[6]](#cite_note-Economist-6) Scientists, business executives, medical practitioners, advertising and [governments](/wiki/Government_database "Government database") alike regularly meet difficulties with large datasets in areas including [Internet searches](/wiki/Web_search_engine "Web search engine"), [fintech](/wiki/Fintech "Fintech"), healthcare analytics, geographic information systems, [urban informatics](/wiki/Urban_informatics "Urban informatics"), and [business informatics](/wiki/Business_informatics "Business informatics"). Scientists encounter limitations in [e-Science](/wiki/E-Science "E-Science") work, including [meteorology](/wiki/Meteorology "Meteorology"), [genomics](/wiki/Genomics "Genomics"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) [connectomics](/wiki/Connectomics "Connectomics"), complex physics simulations, biology, and environmental research.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The size and number of available data sets have grown rapidly as data is collected by devices such as [mobile devices](/wiki/Mobile_device "Mobile device"), cheap and numerous information-sensing [Internet of things](/wiki/Internet_of_things "Internet of things") devices, aerial ([remote sensing](/wiki/Remote_sensing "Remote sensing")) equipment, software logs, [cameras](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera"), microphones, [radio-frequency identification](/wiki/Radio-frequency_identification "Radio-frequency identification") (RFID) readers and [wireless sensor networks](/wiki/Wireless_sensor_networks "Wireless sensor networks").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The world's technological per-capita capacity to store information has roughly doubled every 40 months since the 1980s;[[11]](#cite_note-martinhilbert.net-11) as of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit), every day 2.5 [exabytes](/wiki/Exabyte "Exabyte") (2.17×260 bytes) of data are generated.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Based on an [IDC](/wiki/International_Data_Corporation "International Data Corporation") report prediction, the global data volume was predicted to grow exponentially from 4.4 [zettabytes](/wiki/Zettabyte "Zettabyte") to 44 zettabytes between 2013 and 2020. By 2025, IDC predicts there will be 163 zettabytes of data.[[13]](#cite_note-13) According to IDC, global spending on big data and business analytics (BDA) solutions is estimated to reach $215.7 billion in 2021.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) [Statista](/wiki/Statista "Statista") reported that the global big data market is forecasted to grow to $103 billion by 2027.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 2011 [McKinsey & Company](/wiki/McKinsey_%26_Company "McKinsey & Company") reported, if US healthcare were to use big data creatively and effectively to drive efficiency and quality, the sector could create more than $300 billion in value every year.[[17]](#cite_note-McKinsey2011-17) In the developed economies of Europe, government administrators could save more than €100 billion ($149 billion) in operational efficiency improvements alone by using big data.[[17]](#cite_note-McKinsey2011-17) And users of services enabled by personal-location data could capture $600 billion in consumer surplus.[[17]](#cite_note-McKinsey2011-17) One question for large enterprises is determining who should own big-data initiatives that affect the entire organization.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[Relational database management systems](/wiki/Relational_database_management_system "Relational database management system") and desktop statistical software packages used to visualize data often have difficulty processing and analyzing big data. The processing and analysis of big data may require "massively parallel software running on tens, hundreds, or even thousands of servers".[[19]](#cite_note-19) What qualifies as "big data" varies depending on the capabilities of those analyzing it and their tools. Furthermore, expanding capabilities make big data a moving target. "For some organizations, facing hundreds of [gigabytes](/wiki/Gigabyte "Gigabyte") of data for the first time may trigger a need to reconsider data management options. For others, it may take tens or hundreds of terabytes before data size becomes a significant consideration."[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition")]

The term *big data* has been in use since the 1990s, with some giving credit to [John Mashey](/wiki/John_Mashey "John Mashey") for popularizing the term.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Big data usually includes data sets with sizes beyond the ability of commonly used software tools to [capture](/wiki/Data_acquisition "Data acquisition"), [curate](/wiki/Data_curation "Data curation"), manage, and process data within a tolerable elapsed time.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnijdersMatzatReips2012-23)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Big data philosophy encompasses unstructured, semi-structured and structured data; however, the main focus is on [unstructured data](/wiki/Unstructured_data "Unstructured data").[[24]](#cite_note-Springer_2017-24) Big data "size" is a constantly moving target; as of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit) ranging from a few dozen terabytes to many [zettabytes](/wiki/Zettabyte "Zettabyte") of data.[[25]](#cite_note-Everts-25) Big data requires a set of techniques and technologies with new forms of [integration](/wiki/Data_integration "Data integration") to reveal insights from [datasets](/wiki/Data_set "Data set") that are diverse, complex, and of a massive scale.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Variability is often included as an additional quality of big data.

A 2018 definition states "Big data is where [parallel computing](/wiki/Parallel_computing "Parallel computing") tools are needed to handle data", and notes, "This represents a distinct and clearly defined change in the computer science used, via parallel programming theories, and losses of some of the guarantees and capabilities made by [Codd's relational model](/wiki/Relational_database "Relational database")."[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In a comparative study of big datasets, [Kitchin](/wiki/Rob_Kitchin "Rob Kitchin") and McArdle found that none of the commonly considered characteristics of big data appear consistently across all of the analyzed cases.[[28]](#cite_note-28) For this reason, other studies identified the redefinition of power dynamics in knowledge discovery as the defining trait.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Instead of focusing on the intrinsic characteristics of big data, this alternative perspective pushes forward a relational understanding of the object claiming that what matters is the way in which data is collected, stored, made available and analyzed.

### Big data vs. business intelligence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Big data vs. business intelligence")]

The growing maturity of the concept more starkly delineates the difference between "big data" and "[business intelligence](/wiki/Business_intelligence "Business intelligence")":[[30]](#cite_note-30)

* Business intelligence uses applied mathematics tools and [descriptive statistics](/wiki/Descriptive_statistics "Descriptive statistics") with data with high information density to measure things, detect trends, etc.
* Big data uses mathematical analysis, optimization, [inductive statistics](/wiki/Inductive_statistics "Inductive statistics"), and concepts from [nonlinear system identification](/wiki/Nonlinear_system_identification "Nonlinear system identification")[[31]](#cite_note-SAB1-31) to infer laws (regressions, nonlinear relationships, and causal effects) from large sets of data with low information density[[32]](#cite_note-32) to reveal relationships and dependencies, or to perform predictions of outcomes and behaviors.[[31]](#cite_note-SAB1-31)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[*[promotional source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:RS#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:RS")*]

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Big_Data.png/250px-Big_Data.png)](/wiki/File:Big_Data.png)

This image shows the growth of big data's primary characteristics of volume, velocity, and variety.

Big data can be described by the following characteristics:

Volume
:   The quantity of generated and stored data. The size of the data determines the value and potential insight, and whether it can be considered big data or not. The size of big data is usually larger than terabytes and petabytes.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Variety
:   The type and nature of the data. Earlier technologies like RDBMSs were capable to handle structured data efficiently and effectively. However, the change in type and nature from structured to semi-structured or unstructured challenged the existing tools and technologies. Big data technologies evolved with the prime intention to capture, store, and process the semi-structured and unstructured (variety) data generated with high speed (velocity), and huge in size (volume). Later, these tools and technologies were explored and used for handling structured data also but preferable for storage. Eventually, the processing of structured data was still kept as optional, either using big data or traditional RDBMSs. This helps in analyzing data towards effective usage of the hidden insights exposed from the data collected via social media, log files, sensors, etc. Big data draws from text, images, audio, video; plus it completes missing pieces through [data fusion](/wiki/Data_fusion "Data fusion").

Velocity
:   The speed at which the data is generated and processed to meet the demands and challenges that lie in the path of growth and development. Big data is often available in real-time. Compared to [small data](/wiki/Small_data "Small data"), big data is produced more continually. Two kinds of velocity related to big data are the frequency of generation and the frequency of handling, recording, and publishing.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Veracity
:   The truthfulness or reliability of the data, which refers to the data quality and the data value.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Big data must not only be large in size, but also must be reliable in order to achieve value in the analysis of it. The [data quality](/wiki/Data_quality "Data quality") of captured data can vary greatly, affecting an accurate analysis.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Value
:   The worth in information that can be achieved by the processing and analysis of large datasets. Value also can be measured by an assessment of the other qualities of big data.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Value may also represent the profitability of information that is retrieved from the analysis of big data.

Variability
:   The characteristic of the changing formats, structure, or sources of big data. Big data can include structured, unstructured, or combinations of structured and unstructured data. Big data analysis may integrate raw data from multiple sources. The processing of raw data may also involve transformations of unstructured data to structured data.

Other possible characteristics of big data are:[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Exhaustive
:   Whether the entire system (i.e., ![{\textstyle n}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cc6e1f880981346a604257ebcacdef24c0aca2d6)=all) is captured or recorded or not. Big data may or may not include all the available data from sources.

Fine-grained and uniquely lexical
:   Respectively, the proportion of specific data of each element per element collected and if the element and its characteristics are properly indexed or identified.

Relational
:   If the data collected contains common fields that would enable a conjoining, or meta-analysis, of different data sets.

Extensional
:   If new fields in each element of the data collected can be added or changed easily.

Scalability
:   If the size of the big data storage system can expand rapidly.

## Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Architecture")]

Big data repositories have existed in many forms, often built by corporations with a special need. Commercial vendors historically offered parallel database management systems for big data beginning in the 1990s. For many years, WinterCorp published the largest database report.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[*[promotional source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:RS#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:RS")*]

[Teradata](/wiki/Teradata "Teradata") Corporation in 1984 marketed the parallel processing [DBC 1012](/wiki/DBC_1012 "DBC 1012") system. Teradata systems were the first to store and analyze 1 terabyte of data in 1992. Hard disk drives were 2.5 GB in 1991 so the definition of big data continuously evolves. Teradata installed the first petabyte class RDBMS based system in 2007. As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit), there are a few dozen petabyte class Teradata relational databases installed, the largest of which exceeds 50 PB. Systems up until 2008 were 100% structured relational data. Since then, Teradata has added semi structured data types including [XML](/wiki/XML "XML"), [JSON](/wiki/JSON "JSON"), and [Avro](/wiki/Apache_Avro "Apache Avro").

In 2000, Seisint Inc. (now [LexisNexis Risk Solutions](/wiki/LexisNexis_Risk_Solutions "LexisNexis Risk Solutions")) developed a [C++](/wiki/C%2B%2B "C++")-based distributed platform for data processing and querying known as the [HPCC Systems](/wiki/HPCC_Systems "HPCC Systems") platform. This system automatically partitions, distributes, stores and delivers structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data across multiple commodity servers. Users can write data processing pipelines and queries in a declarative dataflow programming language called ECL. Data analysts working in ECL are not required to define data schemas upfront and can rather focus on the particular problem at hand, reshaping data in the best possible manner as they develop the solution. In 2004, LexisNexis acquired Seisint Inc.[[41]](#cite_note-41) and their high-speed parallel processing platform and successfully used this platform to integrate the data systems of Choicepoint Inc. when they acquired that company in 2008.[[42]](#cite_note-42) In 2011, the HPCC systems platform was open-sourced under the Apache v2.0 License.

[CERN](/wiki/CERN "CERN") and other physics experiments have collected big data sets for many decades, usually analyzed via [high-throughput computing](/wiki/High-throughput_computing "High-throughput computing") rather than the map-reduce architectures usually meant by the current "big data" movement.

In 2004, [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") published a paper on a process called [MapReduce](/wiki/MapReduce "MapReduce") that uses a similar architecture. The MapReduce concept provides a parallel processing model, and an associated implementation was released to process huge amounts of data. With MapReduce, queries are split and distributed across parallel nodes and processed in parallel (the "map" step). The results are then gathered and delivered (the "reduce" step). The framework was very successful,[[43]](#cite_note-43) so others wanted to replicate the algorithm. Therefore, an [implementation](/wiki/Implementation "Implementation") of the MapReduce framework was adopted by an Apache open-source project named "[Hadoop](/wiki/Apache_Hadoop "Apache Hadoop")".[[44]](#cite_note-44) [Apache Spark](/wiki/Apache_Spark "Apache Spark") was developed in 2012 in response to limitations in the MapReduce paradigm, as it adds [in-memory processing](/wiki/In-memory_processing "In-memory processing") and the ability to set up many operations (not just map followed by reducing).

[MIKE2.0](/wiki/MIKE2.0_Methodology "MIKE2.0 Methodology") is an open approach to information management that acknowledges the need for revisions due to big data implications identified in an article titled "Big Data Solution Offering".[[45]](#cite_note-45) The methodology addresses handling big data in terms of useful [permutations](/wiki/Permutation "Permutation") of data sources, [complexity](/wiki/Complexity "Complexity") in interrelationships, and difficulty in deleting (or modifying) individual records.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Studies in 2012 showed that a multiple-layer architecture was one option to address the issues that big data presents. A [distributed parallel](/wiki/List_of_file_systems#Distributed_parallel_file_systems "List of file systems") architecture distributes data across multiple servers; these parallel execution environments can dramatically improve data processing speeds. This type of architecture inserts data into a parallel DBMS, which implements the use of MapReduce and Hadoop frameworks. This type of framework looks to make the processing power transparent to the end-user by using a front-end application server.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The [data lake](/wiki/Data_lake "Data lake") allows an organization to shift its focus from centralized control to a shared model to respond to the changing dynamics of information management. This enables quick segregation of data into the data lake, thereby reducing the overhead time.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

## Technologies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Technologies")]

A 2011 [McKinsey Global Institute](/wiki/McKinsey_%26_Company "McKinsey & Company") report characterizes the main components and ecosystem of big data as follows:[[50]](#cite_note-McKinsey-50)

* Techniques for analyzing data, such as [A/B testing](/wiki/A/B_testing "A/B testing"), [machine learning](/wiki/Machine_learning "Machine learning"), and [natural language processing](/wiki/Natural_language_processing "Natural language processing")
* Big data technologies, like [business intelligence](/wiki/Business_intelligence "Business intelligence"), [cloud computing](/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing"), and [databases](/wiki/Database "Database")
* Visualization, such as charts, graphs, and other displays of the data

Multidimensional big data can also be represented as [OLAP](/wiki/OLAP "OLAP") data cubes or, mathematically, [tensors](/wiki/Tensor "Tensor"). [Array database systems](/wiki/Array_DBMS "Array DBMS") have set out to provide storage and high-level query support on this data type.
Additional technologies being applied to big data include efficient tensor-based computation,[[51]](#cite_note-51) such as [multilinear subspace learning](/wiki/Multilinear_subspace_learning "Multilinear subspace learning"),[[52]](#cite_note-MSLsurvey-52) massively parallel-processing ([MPP](/wiki/Massive_parallel_processing "Massive parallel processing")) databases, [search-based applications](/wiki/Search-based_application "Search-based application"), [data mining](/wiki/Data_mining "Data mining"),[[53]](#cite_note-53) [distributed file systems](/wiki/Distributed_file_system "Distributed file system"), distributed cache (e.g., [burst buffer](/wiki/Burst_buffer "Burst buffer") and [Memcached](/wiki/Memcached "Memcached")), [distributed databases](/wiki/Distributed_database "Distributed database"), [cloud](/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing") and [HPC-based](/wiki/Supercomputer "Supercomputer") infrastructure (applications, storage and computing resources),[[54]](#cite_note-54) and the Internet.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Although, many approaches and technologies have been developed, it still remains difficult to carry out machine learning with big data.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Some [MPP](/wiki/Massive_parallel_processing "Massive parallel processing") relational databases have the ability to store and manage petabytes of data. Implicit is the ability to load, monitor, back up, and optimize the use of the large data tables in the [RDBMS](/wiki/RDBMS "RDBMS").[[56]](#cite_note-56)[*[promotional source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:RS#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:RS")*]

[DARPA](/wiki/DARPA "DARPA")'s [Topological Data Analysis](/wiki/Topological_Data_Analysis "Topological Data Analysis") program seeks the fundamental structure of massive data sets and in 2008 the technology went public with the launch of a company called "Ayasdi".[[57]](#cite_note-57)[*[independent source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Independent_sources "Wikipedia:Independent sources")*]

The practitioners of big data analytics processes are generally hostile to slower shared storage,[[58]](#cite_note-58) preferring direct-attached storage ([DAS](/wiki/Direct-attached_storage "Direct-attached storage")) in its various forms from solid state drive ([SSD](/wiki/SSD "SSD")) to high capacity [SATA](/wiki/Serial_ATA "Serial ATA") disk buried inside parallel processing nodes. The perception of shared storage architectures—[storage area network](/wiki/Storage_area_network "Storage area network") (SAN) and [network-attached storage](/wiki/Network-attached_storage "Network-attached storage") (NAS)— is that they are relatively slow, complex, and expensive. These qualities are not consistent with big data analytics systems that thrive on system performance, commodity infrastructure, and low cost.

Real or near-real-time information delivery is one of the defining characteristics of big data analytics. Latency is therefore avoided whenever and wherever possible. Data in direct-attached memory or disk is good—data on memory or disk at the other end of an [FC](/wiki/Fiber_connector "Fiber connector") [SAN](/wiki/Storage_area_network "Storage area network") connection is not. The cost of an [SAN](/wiki/Storage_area_network "Storage area network") at the scale needed for analytics applications is much higher than other storage techniques.

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Applications")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Exponential_growth_of_datapoints_used_to_train_notable_AI_systems.png/250px-Exponential_growth_of_datapoints_used_to_train_notable_AI_systems.png)](/wiki/File:Exponential_growth_of_datapoints_used_to_train_notable_AI_systems.png)

A graph showing the number of data points used to train notable AI systems from 1950 to 2025[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Big data has increased the demand of information management specialists so much so that [Software AG](/wiki/Software_AG "Software AG"), [Oracle Corporation](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation"), [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"), [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), [SAP](/wiki/SAP_AG "SAP AG"), [EMC](/wiki/EMC_Corporation "EMC Corporation"), [HP](/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard"), and [Dell](/wiki/Dell "Dell") have spent more than $15 billion on software firms specializing in data management and analytics. In 2010, this industry was worth more than $100 billion and was growing at almost 10 percent a year, about twice as fast as the software business as a whole.[[6]](#cite_note-Economist-6)

Developed economies increasingly use data-intensive technologies. There are 4.6 billion mobile-phone subscriptions worldwide, and between 1 billion and 2 billion people accessing the internet.[[6]](#cite_note-Economist-6) Between 1990 and 2005, more than 1 billion people worldwide entered the middle class, which means more people became more literate, which in turn led to information growth. The world's effective capacity to exchange information through telecommunication networks was 281 [petabytes](/wiki/Petabytes "Petabytes") in 1986, 471 petabytes in 1993, 2.2 exabytes in 2000, 65 [exabytes](/wiki/Exabytes "Exabytes") in 2007[[11]](#cite_note-martinhilbert.net-11) and predictions put the amount of internet traffic at 667 exabytes annually by 2014.[[6]](#cite_note-Economist-6) According to one estimate, one-third of the globally stored information is in the form of alphanumeric text and still image data,[[60]](#cite_note-HilbertContent-60) which is the format most useful for most big data applications. This also shows the potential of yet unused data (i.e. in the form of video and audio content).

While many vendors offer off-the-shelf products for big data, experts promote the development of in-house custom-tailored systems if the company has sufficient technical capabilities.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

The use and adoption of big data within governmental processes allows efficiencies in terms of cost, productivity, and innovation,[[62]](#cite_note-62) but comes with flaws. Data analysis often requires multiple parts of government (central and local) to work in collaboration and create new and innovative processes to deliver the desired outcome. A common government organization that makes use of big data is the National Security Administration ([NSA](/wiki/National_Security_Agency "National Security Agency")), which monitors the activities of the Internet constantly in search for potential patterns of suspicious or illegal activities their system may pick up.

[Civil registration and vital statistics](/wiki/Civil_registration_and_vital_statistics "Civil registration and vital statistics") (CRVS) collects all certificates status from birth to death. CRVS is a source of big data for governments.

### International development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: International development")]

Research on the effective usage of information and communication technologies for development (also known as "ICT4D") suggests that big data technology can make important contributions but also present unique challenges to [international development](/wiki/International_development "International development").[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64) Advancements in big data analysis offer cost-effective opportunities to improve decision-making in critical development areas such as health care, employment, [economic productivity](/wiki/Economic_productivity "Economic productivity"), crime, security, and [natural disaster](/wiki/Natural_disaster "Natural disaster") and resource management.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHilbert2016-65)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) Additionally, user-generated data offers new opportunities to give the unheard a voice.[[68]](#cite_note-68) However, longstanding challenges for developing regions such as inadequate technological infrastructure and economic and human resource scarcity exacerbate existing concerns with big data such as privacy, imperfect methodology, and interoperability issues.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHilbert2016-65)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The challenge of "big data for development"[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHilbert2016-65)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] is currently evolving toward the application of this data through machine learning, known as "artificial intelligence for development (AI4D).[[69]](#cite_note-69)

#### Benefits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Benefits")]

A major practical application of big data for development has been "fighting poverty with data".[[70]](#cite_note-70) In 2015, Blumenstock and colleagues estimated predicted poverty and wealth from mobile phone metadata[[71]](#cite_note-71) and in 2016 Jean and colleagues combined satellite imagery and machine learning to predict poverty.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Using digital trace data to study the labor market and the digital economy in Latin America, [Hilbert](/wiki/Martin_Hilbert "Martin Hilbert") and colleagues [[73]](#cite_note-HilbertJobMarket-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74) argue that digital trace data has several benefits such as:

* Thematic coverage: including areas that were previously difficult or impossible to measure
* Geographical coverage: providing sizable and comparable data for almost all countries, including many small countries that usually are not included in international inventories
* Level of detail: providing fine-grained data with many interrelated variables, and new aspects, like network connections
* Timeliness and timeseries: graphs can be produced within days of being collected

#### Challenges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Challenges")]

At the same time, working with digital trace data instead of traditional survey data does not eliminate the traditional challenges involved when working in the field of international quantitative analysis. Priorities change, but the basic discussions remain the same. Among the main challenges are:

* Representativeness. While traditional development statistics is mainly concerned with the representativeness of random survey samples, digital trace data is never a random sample.[[75]](#cite_note-75)
* Generalizability. While observational data always represents this source very well, it only represents what it represents, and nothing more. While it is tempting to generalize from specific observations of one platform to broader settings, this is often very deceptive.
* Harmonization. Digital trace data still requires international harmonization of indicators. It adds the challenge of so-called "data-fusion", the harmonization of different sources.
* Data overload. Analysts and institutions are not used to effectively deal with a large number of variables, which is efficiently done with interactive dashboards. Practitioners still lack a standard workflow that would allow researchers, users and policymakers to efficiently and effectively deal with data.[[73]](#cite_note-HilbertJobMarket-73)

### Finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Finance")]

Big Data is being rapidly adopted in Finance to 1) speed up processing and 2) deliver better, more informed inferences, both internally and to the clients of the financial institutions.[[76]](#cite_note-76) The financial applications of Big Data range from investing decisions and trading (processing volumes of available price data, limit order books, economic data and more, all at the same time), portfolio management (optimizing over an increasingly large array of financial instruments, potentially selected from different asset classes), risk management (credit rating based on extended information), and any other aspect where the data inputs are large.[[77]](#cite_note-77) Big Data has also been a typical concept within the field of [alternative financial service](/wiki/Alternative_financial_service "Alternative financial service"). Some of the major areas involve crowd-funding platforms and crypto currency exchanges.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

### Healthcare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Healthcare")]

Big data analytics has been used in healthcare in providing personalized medicine and [prescriptive analytics](/wiki/Prescriptive_analytics "Prescriptive analytics"), clinical risk intervention and predictive analytics, waste and care variability reduction, automated external and internal reporting of patient data, standardized medical terms and patient registries.[[79]](#cite_note-ref135-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) Some areas of improvement are more aspirational than actually implemented. The level of data generated within [healthcare systems](/wiki/Health_system "Health system") is not trivial. With the added adoption of mHealth, eHealth and wearable technologies the volume of data will continue to increase. This includes [electronic health record](/wiki/Electronic_health_record "Electronic health record") data, imaging data, patient generated data, sensor data, and other forms of difficult to process data. There is now an even greater need for such environments to pay greater attention to data and information quality.[[83]](#cite_note-83) "Big data very often means '[dirty data](/wiki/Dirty_data "Dirty data")' and the fraction of data inaccuracies increases with data volume growth." Human inspection at the big data scale is impossible and there is a desperate need in health service for intelligent tools for accuracy and believability control and handling of information missed.[[84]](#cite_note-Mirkes2016-84) While extensive information in healthcare is now electronic, it fits under the big data umbrella as most is unstructured and difficult to use.[[85]](#cite_note-85) The use of big data in healthcare has raised significant ethical challenges ranging from risks for individual rights, privacy and [autonomy](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy"), to transparency and trust.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Big data in health research is particularly promising in terms of exploratory biomedical research, as data-driven analysis can move forward more quickly than hypothesis-driven research.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Then, trends seen in data analysis can be tested in traditional, hypothesis-driven follow up biological research and eventually clinical research.

A related application sub-area, that heavily relies on big data, within the healthcare field is that of [computer-aided diagnosis](/wiki/Computer-aided_diagnosis "Computer-aided diagnosis") in medicine.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYanaseTriantaphyllou2019-88)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] For instance, for [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy") monitoring it is customary to create 5 to 10 GB of data daily.[[89]](#cite_note-89) Similarly, a single uncompressed image of breast [tomosynthesis](/wiki/Tomosynthesis "Tomosynthesis") averages 450 MB of data.[[90]](#cite_note-90) These are just a few of the many examples where [computer-aided diagnosis](/wiki/Computer-aided_diagnosis "Computer-aided diagnosis") uses big data. For this reason, big data has been recognized as one of the seven key challenges that computer-aided diagnosis systems need to overcome in order to reach the next level of performance.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Education")]

A [McKinsey Global Institute](/wiki/McKinsey_%26_Company "McKinsey & Company") study found a shortage of 1.5 million highly trained data professionals and managers[[50]](#cite_note-McKinsey-50) and a number of universities[[92]](#cite_note-92)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] including [University of Tennessee](/wiki/University_of_Tennessee "University of Tennessee") and [UC Berkeley](/wiki/UC_Berkeley "UC Berkeley"), have created masters programs to meet this demand. Private boot camps have also developed programs to meet that demand, including paid programs like [The Data Incubator](/wiki/The_Data_Incubator "The Data Incubator") or [General Assembly](/wiki/General_Assembly "General Assembly").[[93]](#cite_note-93) In the specific field of marketing, one of the problems stressed by Wedel and Kannan[[94]](#cite_note-94) is that marketing has several sub domains (e.g., advertising, promotions,
product development, branding) that all use different types of data.

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Media")]

To understand how the media uses big data, it is first necessary to provide some context into the mechanism used for media process. It has been suggested by Nick Couldry and Joseph Turow that practitioners in media and advertising approach big data as many actionable points of information about millions of individuals. The industry appears to be moving away from the traditional approach of using specific media environments such as newspapers, magazines, or television shows and instead taps into consumers with technologies that reach targeted people at optimal times in optimal locations. The ultimate aim is to serve or convey, a message or content that is (statistically speaking) in line with the consumer's mindset. For example, publishing environments are increasingly tailoring messages (advertisements) and content (articles) to appeal to consumers that have been exclusively gleaned through various [data-mining](/wiki/Data-mining "Data-mining") activities.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

* Targeting of consumers (for advertising by marketers)[[96]](#cite_note-96)
* Data capture
* [Data journalism](/wiki/Data_journalism "Data journalism"): publishers and journalists use big data tools to provide unique and innovative insights and [infographics](/wiki/Infographic "Infographic").

[Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4"), the British [public-service](/wiki/Public_service_broadcasting_in_the_United_Kingdom "Public service broadcasting in the United Kingdom") television broadcaster, is a leader in the field of big data and [data analysis](/wiki/Data_analysis "Data analysis").[[97]](#cite_note-97)

### Insurance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Insurance")]

Health insurance providers are collecting data on [social "determinants of health"](/wiki/Social_determinants_of_health "Social determinants of health") such as food and [TV consumption](/wiki/Television_consumption "Television consumption"), marital status, clothing size, and purchasing habits, from which they make predictions on health costs, in order to spot health issues in their clients. It is controversial whether these predictions are currently being used for pricing.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

### Internet of things (IoT)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Internet of things (IoT)")]

Main article: [Internet of things](/wiki/Internet_of_things "Internet of things")

Further information: [Edge computing](/wiki/Edge_computing "Edge computing")

Big data and the IoT work in conjunction. Data extracted from IoT devices provides a mapping of device inter-connectivity. Such mappings have been used by the media industry, companies, and governments to more accurately target their audience and increase media efficiency. The IoT is also increasingly adopted as a means of gathering sensory data, and this sensory data has been used in medical,[[99]](#cite_note-99) manufacturing[[100]](#cite_note-100) and transportation[[101]](#cite_note-BigDataIoT16-101) contexts.

[Kevin Ashton](/wiki/Kevin_Ashton "Kevin Ashton"), the digital innovation expert who is credited with coining the term,[[102]](#cite_note-102) defines the Internet of things in this quote: "If we had computers that knew everything there was to know about things—using data they gathered without any help from us—we would be able to track and count everything, and greatly reduce waste, loss, and cost. We would know when things needed replacing, repairing, or recalling, and whether they were fresh or past their best."

### Information technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Information technology")]

Especially since 2015, big data has come to prominence within business operations as a tool to help employees work more efficiently and streamline the collection and distribution of [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology") (IT). The use of big data to resolve IT and [data collection](/wiki/Data_collection "Data collection") issues within an enterprise is called [IT operations analytics](/wiki/IT_operations_analytics "IT operations analytics") (ITOA).[[103]](#cite_note-ITOA1-103) By applying big data principles into the concepts of [machine intelligence](/wiki/Machine_intelligence "Machine intelligence") and deep computing, IT departments can predict potential issues and prevent them.[[103]](#cite_note-ITOA1-103) ITOA businesses offer platforms for [systems management](/wiki/Systems_management "Systems management") that bring [data silos](/wiki/Data_silos "Data silos") together and generate insights from the whole of the system rather than from isolated pockets of data.

### Survey science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Survey science")]

Compared to [survey](/wiki/Survey_methodology "Survey methodology")-based data collection, big data has low cost per data point, applies analysis techniques via [machine learning](/wiki/Machine_learning "Machine learning") and [data mining](/wiki/Data_mining "Data mining"), and includes diverse and new data sources, e.g., registers, social media, apps, and other forms digital data. Since 2018, survey scientists have started to examine how big data and survey science can complement each other to allow researchers and practitioners to improve the production of statistics and its quality. There have been three Big Data Meets Survey Science (BigSurv) conferences in 2018, 2020 (virtual), 2023, and as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit) one conference forthcoming in 2025,[[104]](#cite_note-104) a special issue in the *[Social Science Computer Review](/wiki/Social_Science_Computer_Review "Social Science Computer Review")*,[[105]](#cite_note-105) a special issue in *[Journal of the Royal Statistical Society](/wiki/Journal_of_the_Royal_Statistical_Society "Journal of the Royal Statistical Society")*,[[106]](#cite_note-106) and a special issue in [*EP J Data Science*](/wiki/European_Physical_Journal "European Physical Journal"),[[107]](#cite_note-107) and a book called *Big Data Meets Social Sciences*[[108]](#cite_note-108) edited by [Craig Hill](/wiki/Craig_A._Hill "Craig A. Hill") and five other [Fellows of the American Statistical Association](/wiki/List_of_fellows_of_the_American_Statistical_Association "List of fellows of the American Statistical Association"). In 2021, the founding members of BigSurv received the Warren J. Mitofsky Innovators Award from the [American Association for Public Opinion Research](/wiki/American_Association_for_Public_Opinion_Research "American Association for Public Opinion Research").[[109]](#cite_note-109)

### Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Marketing")]

Big data is notable in marketing due to the constant "datafication"[[110]](#cite_note-:1-110) of everyday consumers of the internet, in which all forms of data are tracked. The datafication of consumers can be defined as quantifying many of or all human behaviors for the purpose of marketing.[[110]](#cite_note-:1-110) The increasingly digital world of rapid datafication makes this idea relevant to marketing because the amount of data constantly grows exponentially. It is predicted to increase from 44 to 163 zettabytes within the span of five years.[[111]](#cite_note-111) The size of big data can often be difficult to navigate for marketers.[[112]](#cite_note-112) As a result, adopters of big data may find themselves at a disadvantage. Algorithmic findings can be difficult to achieve with such large datasets.[[113]](#cite_note-113) Big data in marketing is a highly lucrative tool that can be used for large corporations, its value being as a result of the possibility of predicting significant trends, interests, or statistical outcomes in a consumer-based manner.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

There are three significant factors in the use of big data in marketing:

1. Big data provides customer behavior pattern spotting for marketers, since all human actions are being quantified into readable numbers for marketers to analyze and use for their research.[[115]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-115) In addition, big data can also be seen as a customized product recommendation tool. Specifically, since big data is effective in analyzing customers' purchase behaviors and browsing patterns, this technology can assist companies in promoting specific personalized products to specific customers.[[116]](#cite_note-116)
2. Real-time market responsiveness is important for marketers because of the ability to shift marketing efforts and correct to current trends, which is helpful in maintaining relevance to consumers. This can supply corporations with the information necessary to predict the wants and needs of consumers in advance.[[115]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-115)
3. Data-driven market ambidexterity are being highly fueled by big data.[[115]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-115) New models and algorithms are being developed to make significant predictions about certain economic and social situations.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Case studies")]

### Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Government")]

#### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: China")]

* The Integrated Joint Operations Platform (IJOP, 一体化联合作战平台) is used by the government to monitor the population, particularly [Uyghurs](/wiki/Uyghurs "Uyghurs").[[118]](#cite_note-WP8218-118) [Biometrics](/wiki/Biometrics "Biometrics"), including DNA samples, are gathered through a program of free physicals.[[119]](#cite_note-how022618-119)
* By 2020, China plans to give all its citizens a personal "social credit" score based on how they behave.[[120]](#cite_note-120) The [Social Credit System](/wiki/Social_Credit_System "Social Credit System"), now being piloted in a number of Chinese cities, is considered a form of [mass surveillance](/wiki/Mass_surveillance_in_China "Mass surveillance in China") which uses big data analysis technology.[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122)

#### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: India")]

* Big data analysis was tried out for the [BJP](/wiki/Bharatiya_Janata_Party "Bharatiya Janata Party") to win the 2014 Indian General Election.[[123]](#cite_note-123)
* The [Indian government](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") uses numerous techniques to ascertain how the Indian electorate is responding to government action, as well as ideas for policy augmentation.

#### Israel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Israel")]

* Personalized diabetic treatments can be created through GlucoMe's big data solution.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Examples of uses of big data in public services:

* Data on prescription drugs: by connecting origin, location and the time of each prescription, a research unit was able to exemplify and examine the considerable delay between the release of any given drug, and a UK-wide adaptation of the [National Institute for Health and Care Excellence](/wiki/National_Institute_for_Health_and_Care_Excellence "National Institute for Health and Care Excellence") guidelines. This suggests that new or most up-to-date drugs take some time to filter through to the general patient.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[125]](#cite_note-125)
* Joining up data: a local authority [blended data](/wiki/Data_blending "Data blending") about services, such as road gritting rotas, with services for people at risk, such as [Meals on Wheels](/wiki/Meals_on_Wheels "Meals on Wheels"). The connection of data allowed the local authority to avoid any weather-related delay.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: United States")]

* In 2012, the [Obama administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_Barack_Obama "Presidency of Barack Obama") announced the Big Data Research and Development Initiative, to explore how big data could be used to address important problems faced by the government.[[127]](#cite_note-WH_Big_Data-127) The initiative is composed of 84 different big data programs spread across six departments.[[128]](#cite_note-128)
* Big data analysis played a large role in [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama")'s successful [2012 re-election campaign](/wiki/Barack_Obama_presidential_campaign,_2012 "Barack Obama presidential campaign, 2012").[[129]](#cite_note-infoworld_bigdata-129)

When finished, the facility will be able to handle a large amount of information collected by the NSA over the Internet. The exact amount of storage space is unknown, but more recent sources claim it will be on the order of a few [exabytes](/wiki/Exabyte "Exabyte").[[132]](#cite_note-132)[[133]](#cite_note-133)[[134]](#cite_note-134) This has posed security concerns regarding the anonymity of the data collected.[[135]](#cite_note-135)

### Retail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Retail")]

* [Walmart](/wiki/Walmart "Walmart") handles more than 1 million customer transactions every hour, which are imported into databases estimated to contain more than 2.5 petabytes (2560 terabytes) of data—the equivalent of 167 times the information contained in all the books in the US [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress").[[6]](#cite_note-Economist-6)
* [Windermere Real Estate](/wiki/Windermere_Real_Estate "Windermere Real Estate") uses location information from nearly 100 million drivers to help new home buyers determine their typical drive times to and from work throughout various times of the day.[[136]](#cite_note-136)

### Science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Science")]

* The [Large Hadron Collider](/wiki/Large_Hadron_Collider "Large Hadron Collider") experiments represent about 150 million sensors delivering data 40 million times per second. There are nearly 600 million collisions per second. After filtering and refraining from recording more than 99.99995%[[139]](#cite_note-139) of these streams, there are 1,000 collisions of interest per second.[[140]](#cite_note-140)[[141]](#cite_note-141)[[142]](#cite_note-nature-142)
  + As a result, only working with less than 0.001% of the sensor stream data, the data flow from all four LHC experiments represents 25 petabytes annual rate before replication (as of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit)). This becomes nearly 200 petabytes after replication.
  + If all sensor data were recorded in LHC, the data flow would be extremely hard to work with. The data flow would exceed 150 million petabytes annual rate, or nearly 500 [exabytes](/wiki/Exabyte "Exabyte") per day, before replication. To put the number in perspective, this is equivalent to 500 [quintillion](/wiki/Quintillion "Quintillion") (5×1020) bytes per day, almost 200 times more than all the other sources combined in the world.
* The [Square Kilometre Array](/wiki/Square_Kilometre_Array "Square Kilometre Array") is a radio telescope built of thousands of antennas. It is expected to be operational by 2024. Collectively, these antennas are expected to gather 14 exabytes and store one petabyte per day.[[143]](#cite_note-143)[[144]](#cite_note-144) It is considered one of the most ambitious scientific projects ever undertaken.[[145]](#cite_note-145)
* When the [Sloan Digital Sky Survey](/wiki/Sloan_Digital_Sky_Survey "Sloan Digital Sky Survey") (SDSS) began to collect astronomical data in 2000, it amassed more in its first few weeks than all data collected in the history of astronomy previously. Continuing at a rate of about 200 GB per night, SDSS has amassed more than 140 terabytes of information.[[6]](#cite_note-Economist-6) When the [Large Synoptic Survey Telescope](/wiki/Large_Synoptic_Survey_Telescope "Large Synoptic Survey Telescope"), successor to SDSS, comes online in 2020, its designers expect it to acquire that amount of data every five days.[[6]](#cite_note-Economist-6)
* [Decoding the human genome](/wiki/Human_Genome_Project "Human Genome Project") originally took 10 years to process; now it can be achieved in less than a day. The DNA sequencers have divided the sequencing cost by 10,000 in the last ten years, which is 100 times less expensive than the reduction in cost predicted by [Moore's law](/wiki/Moore%27s_law "Moore's law").[[146]](#cite_note-146)
* The [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") Center for Climate Simulation (NCCS) stores 32 petabytes of climate observations and simulations on the Discover supercomputing cluster.[[147]](#cite_note-147)[[148]](#cite_note-148)
* Google's DNAStack compiles and organizes DNA samples of genetic data from around the world to identify diseases and other medical defects. These fast and exact calculations eliminate any "friction points", or human errors that could be made by one of the numerous science and biology experts working with the DNA. DNAStack, a part of Google Genomics, allows scientists to use the vast sample of resources from Google's search server to scale social experiments that would usually take years, instantly.[[149]](#cite_note-149)[[150]](#cite_note-150)
* [23andme](/wiki/23andme "23andme")'s [DNA database](/wiki/DNA_database "DNA database") contains the genetic information of over 1,000,000 people worldwide.[[151]](#cite_note-151) The company explores selling the "anonymous aggregated genetic data" to other researchers and pharmaceutical companies for research purposes if patients give their consent.[[152]](#cite_note-verge1-152)[[153]](#cite_note-153)[[154]](#cite_note-154)[[155]](#cite_note-155)[[156]](#cite_note-156) Ahmad Hariri, professor of psychology and neuroscience at [Duke University](/wiki/Duke_University "Duke University") who has been using 23andMe in his research since 2009 states that the most important aspect of the company's new service is that it makes genetic research accessible and relatively cheap for scientists.[[152]](#cite_note-verge1-152) A study that identified 15 genome sites linked to depression in 23andMe's database lead to a surge in demands to access the repository with 23andMe fielding nearly 20 requests to access the depression data in the two weeks after publication of the paper.[[157]](#cite_note-157)
* Computational fluid dynamics ([CFD](/wiki/Computational_fluid_dynamics "Computational fluid dynamics")) and hydrodynamic [turbulence](/wiki/Turbulence "Turbulence") research generate massive data sets. The Johns Hopkins Turbulence Databases ([JHTDB](http://turbulence.pha.jhu.edu)) contains over 350 terabytes of spatiotemporal fields from Direct Numerical simulations of various turbulent flows. Such data have been difficult to share using traditional methods such as downloading flat simulation output files. The data within JHTDB can be accessed using "virtual sensors" with various access modes ranging from direct web-browser queries, access through Matlab, Python, Fortran and C programs executing on clients' platforms, to cut out services to download raw data. The data have been used in over 150 scientific publications.

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Sports")]

Big data can be used to improve training and understanding competitors, using sport sensors. It is also possible to predict winners in a match using big data analytics.[[158]](#cite_note-158)
Future performance of players could be predicted as well.[[159]](#cite_note-159) Thus, players' value and salary is determined by data collected throughout the season.[[160]](#cite_note-160)

In [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") races, race cars with hundreds of sensors generate terabytes of data. These sensors collect data points from tire pressure to fuel burn efficiency.[[161]](#cite_note-161)
Based on the data, engineers and data analysts decide whether adjustments should be made in order to win a race. Besides, using big data, race teams try to predict the time they will finish the race beforehand, based on simulations using data collected over the season.[[162]](#cite_note-162)

### Technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Technology")]

* As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit), [eBay.com](/wiki/EBay.com "EBay.com") uses two [data warehouses](/wiki/Data_warehouse "Data warehouse") at 7.5 [petabytes](/wiki/Petabytes "Petabytes") and 40PB as well as a 40PB [Hadoop](/wiki/Hadoop "Hadoop") cluster for search, consumer recommendations, and merchandising.[[163]](#cite_note-163)

* A majorly renowned example of the practice of big data is Amazon. Amazon utilizes data analytics to drive its system for recommendations. Amazon has great success from the portion of sales that derives from the "recommended" section with personalized recommended items to shop.

### COVID-19

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: COVID-19")]

During the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), big data was raised as a way to minimise the impact of the disease.
