## Research activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Research activities")]

Encrypted search and cluster formation in big data were demonstrated in March 2014 at the American Society of Engineering Education. Gautam Siwach engaged at *Tackling the challenges of Big Data* by [MIT Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory](/wiki/MIT_Computer_Science_and_Artificial_Intelligence_Laboratory "MIT Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory") and Amir Esmailpour at the UNH Research Group investigated the key features of big data as the formation of clusters and their interconnections. They focused on the security of big data and the orientation of the term towards the presence of different types of data in an encrypted form at cloud interface by providing the raw definitions and real-time examples within the technology. Moreover, they proposed an approach for identifying the encoding technique to advance towards an expedited search over encrypted text leading to the security enhancements in big data.[[172]](#cite_note-172)

In March 2012, The White House announced a national "Big Data Initiative" that consisted of six federal departments and agencies committing more than $200 million to big data research projects.[[173]](#cite_note-173)

The initiative included a National Science Foundation "Expeditions in Computing" grant of $10 million over five years to the AMPLab[[174]](#cite_note-174) at the University of California, Berkeley.[[175]](#cite_note-175) The AMPLab also received funds from [DARPA](/wiki/DARPA "DARPA"), and over a dozen industrial sponsors and uses big data to attack a wide range of problems from predicting traffic congestion[[176]](#cite_note-176) to fighting cancer.[[177]](#cite_note-177)

The White House Big Data Initiative also included a commitment by the Department of Energy to provide $25 million in funding over five years to establish the Scalable Data Management, Analysis and Visualization (SDAV) Institute,[[178]](#cite_note-178) led by the Energy Department's [Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Berkeley_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory"). The SDAV Institute aims to bring together the expertise of six national laboratories and seven universities to develop new tools to help scientists manage and visualize data on the department's supercomputers.

The U.S. state of [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") announced the Massachusetts Big Data Initiative in May 2012, which provides funding from the state government and private companies to a variety of research institutions.[[179]](#cite_note-179) The [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology") hosts the Intel Science and Technology Center for Big Data in the [MIT Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory](/wiki/MIT_Computer_Science_and_Artificial_Intelligence_Laboratory "MIT Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory"), combining government, corporate, and institutional funding and research efforts.[[180]](#cite_note-180)

The European Commission is funding the two-year-long Big Data Public Private Forum through their Seventh Framework Program to engage companies, academics and other stakeholders in discussing big data issues. The project aims to define a strategy in terms of research and innovation to guide supporting actions from the European Commission in the successful implementation of the big data economy. Outcomes of this project will be used as input for [Horizon 2020](/wiki/Horizon_2020 "Horizon 2020"), their next [framework program](/wiki/Framework_Programmes_for_Research_and_Technological_Development "Framework Programmes for Research and Technological Development").[[181]](#cite_note-181)

The British government announced in March 2014 the founding of the [Alan Turing Institute](/wiki/Alan_Turing_Institute "Alan Turing Institute"), named after the computer pioneer and code-breaker, which will focus on new ways to collect and analyze large data sets.[[182]](#cite_note-182)

At the [University of Waterloo Stratford Campus](/wiki/University_of_Waterloo_Stratford_Campus "University of Waterloo Stratford Campus") Canadian Open Data Experience (CODE) Inspiration Day, participants demonstrated how using data visualization can increase the understanding and appeal of big data sets and communicate their story to the world.[[183]](#cite_note-183)

[Computational social sciences](/wiki/Computational_social_science "Computational social science") – Anyone can use application programming interfaces (APIs) provided by big data holders, such as Google and Twitter, to do research in the social and behavioral sciences.[[184]](#cite_note-pigdata-184) Often these APIs are provided for free.[[184]](#cite_note-pigdata-184) [Tobias Preis](/wiki/Tobias_Preis "Tobias Preis") et al. used [Google Trends](/wiki/Google_Trends "Google Trends") data to demonstrate that Internet users from countries with a higher per capita gross domestic products (GDPs) are more likely to search for information about the future than information about the past. The findings suggest there may be a link between online behaviors and real-world economic indicators.[[185]](#cite_note-185)[[186]](#cite_note-186)[[187]](#cite_note-187) The authors of the study examined Google queries logs made by ratio of the volume of searches for the coming year (2011) to the volume of searches for the previous year (2009), which they call the "[future orientation index](/wiki/Future_orientation_index "Future orientation index")".[[188]](#cite_note-188) They compared the future orientation index to the per capita GDP of each country, and found a strong tendency for countries where Google users inquire more about the future to have a higher GDP.

[Tobias Preis](/wiki/Tobias_Preis "Tobias Preis") and his colleagues Helen Susannah Moat and [H. Eugene Stanley](/wiki/H._Eugene_Stanley "H. Eugene Stanley") introduced a method to identify online precursors for stock market moves, using trading strategies based on search volume data provided by Google Trends.[[189]](#cite_note-189) Their analysis of [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") search volume for 98 terms of varying financial relevance, published in *[Scientific Reports](/wiki/Scientific_Reports "Scientific Reports")*,[[190]](#cite_note-190) suggests that increases in search volume for financially relevant search terms tend to precede large losses in financial markets.[[191]](#cite_note-191)[[192]](#cite_note-192)[[193]](#cite_note-193)[[194]](#cite_note-194)[[195]](#cite_note-195)[[196]](#cite_note-196)[[197]](#cite_note-197)

Big data sets come with algorithmic challenges that previously did not exist. Hence, there is seen by some to be a need to fundamentally change the processing ways.[[198]](#cite_note-198)

### Sampling big data

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Sampling big data")]

A research question that is asked about big data sets is whether it is necessary to look at the full data to draw certain conclusions about the properties of the data or if is a sample is good enough. The name big data itself contains a term related to size and this is an important characteristic of big data. But [sampling](/wiki/Sampling_(statistics) "Sampling (statistics)") enables the selection of right data points from within the larger data set to estimate the characteristics of the whole population. In manufacturing different types of sensory data such as acoustics, vibration, pressure, current, voltage, and controller data are available at short time intervals. To predict downtime it may not be necessary to look at all the data but a sample may be sufficient. Big data can be broken down by various data point categories such as demographic, psychographic, behavioral, and transactional data. With large sets of data points, marketers are able to create and use more customized segments of consumers for more strategic targeting.

## Critique

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Critique")]

Critiques of the big data paradigm come in two flavors: those that question the implications of the approach itself, and those that question the way it is currently done.[[199]](#cite_note-Kimble_and_Milolidakis_(2015)-199) One approach to this criticism is the field of [critical data studies](/wiki/Critical_data_studies "Critical data studies").

### Critiques of the big data paradigm

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Critiques of the big data paradigm")]

"A crucial problem is that we do not know much about the underlying empirical micro-processes that lead to the emergence of the[se] typical network characteristics of Big Data."[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESnijdersMatzatReips2012-23)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] In their critique, Snijders, Matzat, and [Reips](/wiki/Ulf-Dietrich_Reips "Ulf-Dietrich Reips") point out that often very strong assumptions are made about mathematical properties that may not at all reflect what is really going on at the level of micro-processes. Mark Graham has leveled broad critiques at [Chris Anderson](/wiki/Chris_Anderson_(writer) "Chris Anderson (writer)")'s assertion that big data will spell the end of theory:[[200]](#cite_note-200) focusing in particular on the notion that big data must always be contextualized in their social, economic, and political contexts.[[201]](#cite_note-201) Even as companies invest eight- and nine-figure sums to derive insight from information streaming in from suppliers and customers, less than 40% of employees have sufficiently mature processes and skills to do so. To overcome this insight deficit, big data, no matter how comprehensive or well analyzed, must be complemented by "big judgment", according to an article in the *[Harvard Business Review](/wiki/Harvard_Business_Review "Harvard Business Review")*.[[202]](#cite_note-202)

Much in the same line, it has been pointed out that the decisions based on the analysis of big data are inevitably "informed by the world as it was in the past, or, at best, as it currently is".[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHilbert2016-65)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Fed by a large number of data on past experiences, algorithms can predict future development if the future is similar to the past.[[203]](#cite_note-HilbertTEDx-203) If the system's dynamics of the future change (if it is not a [stationary process](/wiki/Stationary_process "Stationary process")), the past can say little about the future. In order to make predictions in changing environments, it would be necessary to have a thorough understanding of the systems dynamic, which requires theory.[[203]](#cite_note-HilbertTEDx-203) As a response to this critique Alemany Oliver and Vayre suggest to use "abductive reasoning as a first step in the research process in order to bring context to consumers' digital traces and make new theories emerge".[[204]](#cite_note-204) Additionally, it has been suggested to combine big data approaches with computer simulations, such as [agent-based models](/wiki/Agent-based_model "Agent-based model")[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHilbert2016-65)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] and [complex systems](/wiki/Complex_systems "Complex systems"). Agent-based models are increasingly getting better in predicting the outcome of social complexities of even unknown future scenarios through computer simulations that are based on a collection of mutually interdependent algorithms.[[205]](#cite_note-205)[[206]](#cite_note-206) Finally, the use of multivariate methods that probe for the latent structure of the data, such as [factor analysis](/wiki/Factor_analysis "Factor analysis") and [cluster analysis](/wiki/Cluster_analysis "Cluster analysis"), have proven useful as analytic approaches that go well beyond the bi-variate approaches (e.g. [contingency tables](/wiki/Contingency_table "Contingency table")) typically employed with smaller data sets.

In health and biology, conventional scientific approaches are based on experimentation. For these approaches, the limiting factor is the relevant data that can confirm or refute the initial hypothesis.[[207]](#cite_note-207) A new postulate is accepted now in biosciences: the information provided by the data in huge volumes ([omics](/wiki/Omics "Omics")) without prior hypothesis is complementary and sometimes necessary to conventional approaches based on experimentation.[[208]](#cite_note-208)[[209]](#cite_note-209) In the massive approaches it is the formulation of a relevant hypothesis to explain the data that is the limiting factor.[[210]](#cite_note-210) The search logic is reversed and the limits of induction ("Glory of Science and Philosophy scandal", [C. D. Broad](/wiki/C._D._Broad "C. D. Broad"), 1926) are to be considered.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Privacy](/wiki/Consumer_privacy "Consumer privacy") advocates are concerned about the threat to privacy represented by increasing storage and integration of [personally identifiable information](/wiki/Personally_identifiable_information "Personally identifiable information"); expert panels have released various policy recommendations to conform practice to expectations of privacy.[[211]](#cite_note-211) The misuse of big data in several cases by media, companies, and even the government has allowed for abolition of trust in almost every fundamental institution holding up society.[[212]](#cite_note-212)

Barocas and Nissenbaum argue that one way of protecting individual users is by being informed about the types of information being collected, with whom it is shared, under what constraints and for what purposes.[[213]](#cite_note-213)

### Critiques of the "V" model

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Critiques of the \"V\" model")]

The "V" model of big data is concerning as it centers around computational scalability and lacks in a loss around the perceptibility and understandability of information. This led to the framework of [cognitive big data](/w/index.php?title=Cognitive_big_data&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cognitive big data (page does not exist)"), which characterizes big data applications according to:[[214]](#cite_note-CSO_1-214)

* Data completeness: understanding of the non-obvious from data
* Data correlation, causation, and predictability: causality as not essential requirement to achieve predictability
* Explainability and interpretability: humans desire to understand and accept what they understand, where algorithms do not cope with this
* Level of [automated decision-making](/wiki/Automated_decision-making "Automated decision-making"): algorithms that support automated decision making and algorithmic self-learning

### Critiques of novelty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Critiques of novelty")]

Large data sets have been analyzed by computing machines for well over a century, including the US census analytics performed by [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM")'s punch-card machines which computed statistics including means and variances of populations across the whole continent. In more recent decades, science experiments such as [CERN](/wiki/CERN "CERN") have produced data on similar scales to current commercial "big data". However, science experiments have tended to analyze their data using specialized custom-built [high-performance computing](/wiki/High-performance_computing "High-performance computing") (super-computing) clusters and grids, rather than clouds of cheap commodity computers as in the current commercial wave, implying a difference in both culture and technology stack.

### Critiques of big data execution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Critiques of big data execution")]

[Ulf-Dietrich Reips](/wiki/Ulf-Dietrich_Reips "Ulf-Dietrich Reips") and Uwe Matzat wrote in 2014 that big data had become a "fad" in scientific research.[[184]](#cite_note-pigdata-184) Researcher [danah boyd](/wiki/Danah_boyd "Danah boyd") has raised concerns about the use of big data in science neglecting principles such as choosing a [representative sample](/wiki/Sampling_(statistics) "Sampling (statistics)") by being too concerned about handling the huge amounts of data.[[215]](#cite_note-danah-215) This approach may lead to results that have a [bias](/wiki/Bias_(statistics) "Bias (statistics)") in one way or another.[[216]](#cite_note-216) Integration across heterogeneous data resources—some that might be considered big data and others not—presents formidable logistical as well as analytical challenges, but many researchers argue that such integrations are likely to represent the most promising new frontiers in science.[[217]](#cite_note-217)
In the provocative article "Critical Questions for Big Data",[[218]](#cite_note-danah2-218) the authors title big data a part of [mythology](/wiki/Mythology "Mythology"): "large data sets offer a higher form of intelligence and knowledge [...], with the aura of truth, objectivity, and accuracy". Users of big data are often "lost in the sheer volume of numbers", and "working with Big Data is still subjective, and what it quantifies does not necessarily have a closer claim on objective truth".[[218]](#cite_note-danah2-218) Recent developments in BI domain, such as pro-active reporting especially target improvements in the usability of big data, through automated [filtering](/wiki/Filter_(software) "Filter (software)") of [non-useful data and correlations](/wiki/Spurious_relationship "Spurious relationship").[[219]](#cite_note-Big_Decisions_White_Paper-219) Big structures are full of spurious correlations[[220]](#cite_note-220) either because of non-causal coincidences ([law of truly large numbers](/wiki/Law_of_truly_large_numbers "Law of truly large numbers")), solely nature of big randomness[[221]](#cite_note-221) ([Ramsey theory](/wiki/Ramsey_theory "Ramsey theory")), or existence of [non-included factors](/wiki/Confounding_factor "Confounding factor") so the hope, of early experimenters to make large databases of numbers "speak for themselves" and revolutionize scientific method, is questioned.[[222]](#cite_note-222) [Catherine Tucker](/wiki/Catherine_Tucker "Catherine Tucker") has pointed to "hype" around big data, writing "By itself, big data is unlikely to be valuable." The article explains: "The many contexts where data is cheap relative to the cost of retaining talent to process it, suggests that processing skills are more important than data itself in creating value for a firm."[[223]](#cite_note-223)

Big data analysis is often shallow compared to analysis of smaller data sets.[[224]](#cite_note-kdnuggets-berchthold-224) In many big data projects, there is no large data analysis happening, but the challenge is the [extract, transform, load](/wiki/Extract,_transform,_load "Extract, transform, load") part of data pre-processing.[[224]](#cite_note-kdnuggets-berchthold-224)

Big data is a [buzzword](/wiki/Buzzword "Buzzword") and a "vague term",[[225]](#cite_note-225)[[226]](#cite_note-ft-harford-226) but at the same time an "obsession"[[226]](#cite_note-ft-harford-226) with entrepreneurs, consultants, scientists, and the media. Big data showcases such as [Google Flu Trends](/wiki/Google_Flu_Trends "Google Flu Trends") failed to deliver good predictions in recent years, overstating the flu outbreaks by a factor of two. Similarly, [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") and election predictions solely based on Twitter were more often off than on target.
Big data often poses the same challenges as small data; adding more data does not solve problems of bias, but may emphasize other problems. In particular data sources such as Twitter are not representative of the overall population, and results drawn from such sources may then lead to wrong conclusions. [Google Translate](/wiki/Google_Translate "Google Translate")—which is based on big data statistical analysis of text—does a good job at translating web pages. However, results from specialized domains may be dramatically skewed.
On the other hand, big data may also introduce new problems, such as the [multiple comparisons problem](/wiki/Multiple_comparisons_problem "Multiple comparisons problem"): simultaneously testing a large set of hypotheses is likely to produce many false results that mistakenly appear significant.
Ioannidis argued that "most published research findings are false"[[227]](#cite_note-Ioannidis-227) due to essentially the same effect: when many scientific teams and researchers each perform many experiments (i.e. process a big amount of scientific data; although not with big data technology), the likelihood of a "significant" result being false grows fast – even more so, when only positive results are published.
Furthermore, big data analytics results are only as good as the model on which they are predicated. In an example, big data took part in attempting to predict the results of the 2016 U.S. presidential election[[228]](#cite_note-228) with varying degrees of success.

### Critiques of big data policing and surveillance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Big_data&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Critiques of big data policing and surveillance")]

Big data has been used in policing and surveillance by institutions like [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_the_United_States "Law enforcement in the United States") and corporations (see: [corporate surveillance](/wiki/Corporate_surveillance "Corporate surveillance") and [surveillance capitalism](/wiki/Surveillance_capitalism "Surveillance capitalism")).[[229]](#cite_note-229) Due to the less visible nature of data-based surveillance as compared to traditional methods of policing, objections to big data policing are less likely to arise. According to Sarah Brayne's *Big Data Surveillance: The Case of Policing*,[[230]](#cite_note-230) big data policing can reproduce existing [societal inequalities](/wiki/Social_inequality "Social inequality") in three ways:

* Placing people under increased surveillance by using the justification of a mathematical and therefore unbiased algorithm
* Increasing the scope and number of people that are subject to law enforcement tracking and exacerbating existing [racial overrepresentation](/wiki/Race_in_the_United_States_criminal_justice_system#Racial_inequality_in_incarceration "Race in the United States criminal justice system") in the criminal justice system
* Encouraging members of society to abandon interactions with institutions that would create a digital trace, thus creating obstacles to social inclusion

If these potential problems are not corrected or regulated, the effects of big data policing may continue to shape societal hierarchies. Conscientious usage of big data policing could prevent individual level biases from becoming institutional biases, Brayne also notes.
